words.simpleMeaning: used for something near

sentences.office: This document needs your signature.
sentences.interview: This role perfectly matches my skills.
sentences.business: This proposal is worth considering.
sentences.formal: This matter requires immediate attention.

words.usageNote: 'This' is used for singular objects/people that are close to the speaker.

words.simpleMeaning: used for something far

sentences.office: That report was submitted yesterday.
sentences.interview: That experience shaped my career.
sentences.business: That deal was very profitable.
sentences.formal: That policy needs to be revised.

words.usageNote: 'That' is used for singular objects/people that are far from the speaker, or to refer to something already mentioned.

words.simpleMeaning: plural form of 'this' for nearby things

sentences.office: These files need to be updated.
sentences.interview: These skills are very relevant.
sentences.business: These clients are our top priority.
sentences.formal: These regulations must be followed.

words.usageNote: 'These' is the plural form of 'this', used for multiple objects/people that are near the speaker.

words.simpleMeaning: plural form of 'that' for faraway things

sentences.office: Those employees are very dedicated.
sentences.interview: Those challenges made me stronger.
sentences.business: Those projects were very successful.
sentences.formal: Those regulations are no longer valid.

words.usageNote: 'Those' is the plural form of 'that', used for multiple objects/people that are far from the speaker.

words.simpleMeaning: a written or printed work

sentences.office: That book on the shelf is mine.
sentences.interview: This book helped me prepare well.
sentences.business: Those reference books are outdated.
sentences.formal: These books are part of the library collection.
sentences.informal: This book is so good, I can't put it down!

words.usageNote: 'Book' as a verb means to reserve (book a ticket). As a noun it refers to a written work.

words.simpleMeaning: a writing instrument

sentences.office: Can you pass me that pen?
sentences.interview: Please sign with this pen.
sentences.business: These pens are branded for the event.
sentences.formal: That pen belongs to the management.

words.usageNote: 'Pen' as a verb means to write something. As a noun it is a writing instrument.

words.simpleMeaning: a flat-topped furniture piece

sentences.daily: This table is made of wood.
sentences.office: Those tables in the conference room are new.
sentences.interview: Sit at that table for the interview.
sentences.business: These tables will be used for the exhibition.
sentences.formal: That table has been reserved for the guests.

words.usageNote: 'Table a proposal' means to postpone it in American English but to present it in British English.

words.simpleMeaning: a seat with a back for one person

sentences.office: That chair belongs to the manager.
sentences.interview: Please take this chair.
sentences.business: Those chairs need to be arranged before the meeting.
sentences.formal: The chairperson will sit in that chair.

words.usageNote: 'Chair' as a verb means to lead a meeting or committee.

words.simpleMeaning: a hinged barrier for an entrance

sentences.office: That door leads to the conference room.
sentences.interview: Please close this door behind you.
sentences.business: Those doors are always kept locked.
sentences.formal: The door to this office is always open for consultation.

words.usageNote: 'Door' is only used as a noun. 'Open the door' and 'close the door' are common expressions.

words.simpleMeaning: an opening in a wall to let in light

sentences.daily: This window lets in a lot of light.
sentences.office: That window is facing the parking area.
sentences.interview: Please sit near that window.
sentences.business: Those windows need to be cleaned before the clients arrive.
sentences.formal: This window was installed last month.
sentences.informal: Open that window, it's so hot!

words.usageNote: 'Window' in computing also means an application panel on screen.

words.simpleMeaning: an electronic device for processing data

sentences.office: That computer has the latest software.
sentences.interview: I am proficient in using this type of computer.
sentences.business: Those computers need upgrading.
sentences.formal: This computer stores all confidential data.
sentences.informal: This computer is so slow today!

words.usageNote: 'Computer' is always used as a singular countable noun. 'These computers' for plural.

words.simpleMeaning: a device used to make calls

sentences.daily: This phone has a great camera.
sentences.office: That phone on the desk is for official use only.
sentences.interview: Please keep your phone on silent during the interview.
sentences.business: Those phones are company property.
sentences.formal: This phone line is dedicated to customer service.
sentences.informal: Wow, this phone is brand new!

words.usageNote: 'Phone' as a verb means to call someone. 'I'll phone you later.'

words.simpleMeaning: a container for carrying things

sentences.office: That bag contains all the meeting documents.
sentences.interview: Please leave your bag at the reception.
sentences.business: Those bags were shipped to the warehouse.
sentences.formal: This bag was found at the premises.
sentences.informal: I love that bag you're carrying!

words.usageNote: 'Bag a seat' means to grab/reserve a seat informally.

words.simpleMeaning: a garment worn on the upper body

sentences.office: That white shirt looks very professional.
sentences.interview: Wear this shirt for the interview tomorrow.
sentences.business: Those branded shirts are for our team.
sentences.formal: This shirt must be ironed before the meeting.
sentences.informal: That shirt is totally my style!

words.usageNote: In formal settings, a collared shirt is preferred. 'This shirt doesn't fit' = size problem.

words.simpleMeaning: a one-piece garment for women

sentences.office: That dress is too casual for the office.
sentences.interview: She wore that elegant dress to the interview.
sentences.business: Those dresses are part of our new collection.
sentences.formal: This dress code is mandatory for all staff.
sentences.informal: Omg, I love this dress on you!

words.usageNote: 'Dress up' means to wear formal clothes. 'Dress down' means to wear casual clothes.

words.simpleMeaning: footwear for protection and style

sentences.office: Those formal shoes match your suit perfectly.
sentences.interview: These shoes look very professional.
sentences.business: Those shoes were manufactured in Italy.
sentences.formal: These shoes must be polished before the presentation.
sentences.informal: I need to buy those shoes!

words.usageNote: 'Shoes' is always plural. 'A pair of shoes' for singular reference.

words.simpleMeaning: a motor vehicle for transport

sentences.office: That car in the parking lot belongs to the CEO.
sentences.interview: I came by that car.
sentences.business: Those company cars are available for client visits.
sentences.formal: This car has been assigned to the department head.

words.usageNote: In India, 'car' and 'gaadi' are used interchangeably in conversation.

words.simpleMeaning: a building where people live

sentences.office: The company is housed in that building.
sentences.interview: I live in this house near the office.
sentences.business: Those houses are part of the residential project.
sentences.formal: This house was built in 1985.

words.usageNote: 'House' vs 'home': House is the physical structure; home has an emotional meaning.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where children are educated

sentences.office: That school alumni run our company.
sentences.interview: I studied in that school for ten years.
sentences.business: Those schools are our key clients.
sentences.formal: This school follows the CBSE curriculum.
sentences.informal: This school canteen is the best!

words.usageNote: 'Go to school' without article means attending as a student. 'This school' with article points to a specific school.

words.simpleMeaning: a place of work

sentences.office: That office has a great work culture.
sentences.interview: This office is where you will be working.
sentences.business: Those offices are located in the business district.
sentences.formal: This office will be shifted to the new building next month.
sentences.informal: This office has the best coffee machine!

words.usageNote: 'Office hours' means working hours. 'In the office' vs 'at the office' — both are acceptable.

words.simpleMeaning: a person who teaches others

sentences.office: That teacher has 20 years of experience.
sentences.interview: I used to be that kind of teacher.
sentences.business: Those teachers are training our corporate team.
sentences.formal: This teacher has been awarded for excellence.
sentences.informal: This teacher is literally my favorite!

words.usageNote: 'Teacher' is used for school-level education. 'Professor' is used for university level.

words.simpleMeaning: a person who studies

sentences.office: That student internship program was successful.
sentences.interview: As a student, I learned a lot.
sentences.business: Those students are our target audience.
sentences.formal: This student has secured first rank.
sentences.informal: That student always has all the answers!

words.usageNote: 'Student' is used for higher education. 'Pupil' is more common for school students in British English.

words.simpleMeaning: a person you know well and like

sentences.office: That colleague has become a good friend.
sentences.interview: My friend recommended this company.
sentences.business: Those friends of mine are business partners.
sentences.formal: This friend has been a great support system.

words.usageNote: 'Friend' is different from 'acquaintance' — you know a friend well. 'This is my friend' = introduction.

words.simpleMeaning: a female parent

sentences.office: That mother manages work and home beautifully.
sentences.interview: My mother inspired me to pursue this career.
sentences.business: Those mothers formed a support group.
sentences.formal: This mother has raised three successful children.
sentences.informal: This is the best mom ever!

words.usageNote: 'Mother' as a verb means to care for someone like a mother. Used in introduction: 'This is my mother.'

words.simpleMeaning: a male parent

sentences.office: His father founded this company.
sentences.interview: My father taught me discipline.
sentences.business: That father-son duo runs a successful business.
sentences.formal: This father has set an example for all.
sentences.informal: My dad is literally the coolest!

words.usageNote: 'Father' is more formal than 'dad'. Used in introductions: 'That is my father.'

words.simpleMeaning: a female sibling

sentences.office: That woman is my sister who also works here.
sentences.interview: My sister encouraged me to apply.
sentences.business: Those sisters co-own the business.
sentences.formal: This is my sister, Priya.

words.usageNote: 'Sister' can also refer to a nun or a senior nurse in British English.

words.simpleMeaning: a male sibling

sentences.office: That is my brother who is also my business partner.
sentences.interview: My brother works in this field too.
sentences.business: Those brothers manage the family business.
sentences.formal: This is my elder brother, Rahul.

words.usageNote: 'Brother' in informal English also means a close male friend. In introduction: 'This is my brother.'

words.simpleMeaning: a round red or green fruit

sentences.office: Would you like this apple?
sentences.interview: Staying healthy — an apple a day!
sentences.business: Those apples are from our organic farm.
sentences.formal: These apples were imported from Himachal Pradesh.

words.usageNote: 'Apple' starts with a vowel sound, so use 'an apple'. 'Is this an apple?' = A1 demonstrative question.

words.simpleMeaning: a tropical yellow-orange fruit

sentences.office: These mangoes are from our client's farm.
sentences.interview: That mango season was when I started this job.
sentences.business: Those mangoes are being exported to Dubai.
sentences.formal: These mangoes are of Alphonso variety.
sentences.informal: Omg, this mango is literally heaven!

words.usageNote: In India, mango is often called the 'king of fruits'. 'Are these mangoes sweet?' is a common market question.

words.simpleMeaning: a clear liquid essential for life

sentences.office: Is this water purified?
sentences.interview: May I have a glass of water?
sentences.business: Those water purifiers are installed in all our branches.
sentences.formal: This water is safe for drinking.
sentences.informal: Can you get me some of that water?

words.usageNote: 'Water' is uncountable, so we say 'this water', not 'these waters'. 'A glass of water' adds a container to make it countable.

words.simpleMeaning: things we eat for nourishment

sentences.office: That cafeteria food is not healthy.
sentences.interview: This company provides free food.
sentences.business: Those food products are our bestsellers.
sentences.formal: This food has been prepared hygienically.

words.usageNote: 'Food' is usually uncountable (this food). When counting types: 'these foods are healthy'.

words.simpleMeaning: a structure with walls and a roof

sentences.office: That building is our new headquarters.
sentences.interview: The interview is in this building on the 5th floor.
sentences.business: Those buildings are commercial properties.
sentences.formal: This building was constructed in 2005.

words.usageNote: 'Building' as a noun refers to a structure. As a gerund: 'Building a house takes time.'

words.simpleMeaning: a path for vehicles and people

sentences.office: That road leads directly to the office.
sentences.interview: Take this road to reach our building.
sentences.business: Those roads are being repaired.
sentences.formal: This road has been declared a national highway.
sentences.informal: OMG this road has so many potholes!

words.usageNote: 'Road' is more general. 'Street' is in a city/town. 'Highway' is a fast road. 'Take this road' = direction giving.

words.simpleMeaning: a tall plant with a trunk and branches

sentences.office: Those trees in the garden make the office beautiful.
sentences.interview: That company planted this tree 50 years ago.
sentences.business: These trees are being used for commercial timber.
sentences.formal: This tree is a protected species.
sentences.informal: That tree looks like it's been there forever!

words.usageNote: 'Tree' is used with demonstratives: 'this tree', 'those trees'. 'Up a tree' is an idiom meaning in difficulty.

words.simpleMeaning: the colorful part of a plant

sentences.office: Those flowers on the reception desk are fresh.
sentences.interview: She brought these flowers for the interview room.
sentences.business: Those flower arrangements are from our partner.
sentences.formal: These flowers are part of the ceremonial decoration.
sentences.informal: I love these flowers so much!

words.usageNote: 'Flower' as a verb means to produce flowers or to flourish. 'These flowers bloom every spring.'

words.simpleMeaning: a very high natural landform

sentences.daily: Those mountains are covered with snow.
sentences.office: Our office is near that mountain range.
sentences.interview: That mountain trek was my biggest challenge.
sentences.business: Those mountain regions are our target market.
sentences.formal: These mountains are protected under environmental law.
sentences.informal: Look at those mountains, they're stunning!

words.usageNote: 'Mountain' vs 'hill': Mountains are much higher. 'Make a mountain out of a molehill' is an idiom.

words.simpleMeaning: a large natural stream of water

sentences.office: Our factory is near that river.
sentences.interview: Growing up near this river taught me patience.
sentences.business: Those river valleys are ideal for agriculture.
sentences.formal: This river is the source of water for the city.

words.usageNote: Rivers are usually referred to with 'the' — 'the Ganga', 'this river'. Swimming in this river may be dangerous.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where sick people are treated

sentences.daily: That hospital is very far from here.
sentences.office: This hospital is our client for medical supplies.
sentences.interview: I worked in that hospital for three years.
sentences.business: Those hospitals need our medical equipment.
sentences.formal: This hospital is accredited by NABH.
sentences.informal: That hospital has really good doctors!

words.usageNote: 'In hospital' (British) vs 'in the hospital' (American). 'This hospital' points to a specific one.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where things are bought and sold

sentences.office: That market segment is growing rapidly.
sentences.interview: I have experience in that market.
sentences.business: Those markets are our primary revenue sources.
sentences.formal: This market research was commissioned last quarter.
sentences.informal: Let's go to that market this evening!

words.usageNote: 'Market' as a verb means to promote/sell a product. 'Stock market' is different from a local market.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where goods are sold

sentences.daily: This shop sells the best samosas.
sentences.office: That shop supplies our office stationery.
sentences.interview: My first job was in that shop.
sentences.business: Those shops are our retail partners.
sentences.formal: This shop is registered under GST.

words.usageNote: 'Go shopping' vs 'go to the shop'. 'Shop around' = look at many places before buying.

words.simpleMeaning: a place where food is served

sentences.office: That restaurant caters to our office.
sentences.interview: We had the meeting in that restaurant.
sentences.business: Those restaurants are our food delivery clients.
sentences.formal: This restaurant has received the best hygiene award.
sentences.informal: This restaurant is my absolute favourite!

words.usageNote: 'Restaurant' always has a definite article when specific: 'this restaurant', 'that restaurant'.

words.simpleMeaning: a building offering rooms and meals

sentences.office: Our client is staying in this hotel.
sentences.interview: The interview was held at that hotel.
sentences.business: Those hotels are part of our hospitality chain.
sentences.formal: This hotel has a five-star rating.

words.usageNote: 'Hotel' with vowel sound — 'a hotel' (not 'an hotel'). 'Is that hotel far?' = common question.

words.simpleMeaning: a large vehicle carrying many passengers

sentences.daily: This bus goes to the railway station.
sentences.office: That bus is the office shuttle.
sentences.interview: Take this bus to reach the venue.
sentences.business: Those buses transport our factory workers.
sentences.formal: This bus route has been newly introduced.
sentences.informal: Hurry up, that bus is about to leave!

words.usageNote: 'Bus' as a verb in American English means to transport. 'Take this bus' = direction. 'Miss the bus' = be late.

words.simpleMeaning: a vehicle running on rails

sentences.daily: That train is always on time.
sentences.office: This train connects the city to the IT park.
sentences.interview: I took this train to reach the interview.
sentences.business: Those trains carry goods for our supply chain.
sentences.formal: This train route was inaugurated by the minister.

words.usageNote: 'Train' as a verb means to practice or teach. 'This train departs at 6 PM' = timetable use.

words.simpleMeaning: a medical professional who treats illness

sentences.office: That doctor comes to our office for regular health checkups.
sentences.interview: I have an appointment with that doctor.
sentences.business: Those doctors are our key customers.
sentences.formal: This doctor is the head of cardiology.
sentences.informal: That doctor is so nice and friendly!

words.usageNote: 'Doctor' as a verb means to falsify (doctor the records). Always use 'the doctor' or 'this doctor' for specific reference.

words.simpleMeaning: a substance used to treat illness

sentences.office: Those medicines are kept in the first aid kit.
sentences.interview: My experience is in pharmaceutical medicine.
sentences.business: These medicines are distributed to rural areas.
sentences.formal: This medicine requires a doctor's prescription.
sentences.informal: Ugh, this medicine tastes so bitter!

words.usageNote: 'Medicine' is uncountable for the field. As countable: 'these medicines' = specific pills/tablets.

words.simpleMeaning: a difficult situation that needs solving

sentences.office: That problem in the system needs fixing.
sentences.interview: I solve this kind of problem regularly.
sentences.business: Those problems delayed the project.
sentences.formal: This problem was reported to management.

words.usageNote: 'No problem!' = It's okay. 'This problem needs attention' = demonstrative + noun. 'Problem-solving' is a key professional skill.

words.simpleMeaning: the answer to a problem

sentences.office: That solution worked perfectly.
sentences.interview: I came up with this solution single-handedly.
sentences.business: Those solutions are already implemented.
sentences.formal: This solution has been approved by management.
sentences.informal: Finally, that's the solution I was looking for!

words.usageNote: 'This solution' = specific answer. 'Solution to this problem' is the typical pairing.

words.simpleMeaning: a thought or plan in the mind

sentences.office: That idea saved us a lot of time.
sentences.interview: This idea shows my creative thinking.
sentences.business: Those ideas were presented in the boardroom.
sentences.formal: This idea has been patented.
sentences.informal: Oh wow, that idea is genius!

words.usageNote: 'Idea' starts with a vowel — 'an idea'. With demonstrative: 'this idea' (no article needed). 'Have an idea' = think of something.

words.simpleMeaning: recently received information

sentences.office: That news about the merger surprised everyone.
sentences.interview: I keep updated with this kind of news.
sentences.business: Those news items affected our stock price.
sentences.formal: This news has been officially confirmed.

words.usageNote: 'News' is ALWAYS singular in English — 'this news', never 'these news'. 'Good news' vs 'bad news'.

words.simpleMeaning: a chance to do something

sentences.office: That opportunity changed my career.
sentences.interview: This opportunity aligns with my goals.
sentences.business: Those opportunities were not fully utilized.
sentences.formal: This opportunity should be seized immediately.

words.usageNote: 'This is a golden opportunity' = very good chance. 'Seize this opportunity' = take advantage of it.

words.simpleMeaning: something done incorrectly

sentences.office: That mistake in the report needs correction.
sentences.interview: I learned a lot from that mistake.
sentences.business: Those mistakes delayed the product launch.
sentences.formal: This mistake must not be repeated.

words.usageNote: 'Make a mistake' is the correct collocation — not 'do a mistake'. 'This mistake' = specific error.

words.simpleMeaning: a choice made after thinking

sentences.office: That decision was made without consulting us.
sentences.interview: This decision helped me grow professionally.
sentences.business: Those decisions were purely data-driven.
sentences.formal: This decision has been ratified by the board.
sentences.informal: Trust me, this decision is the right one!

words.usageNote: 'Make a decision' = decide. 'This is a tough decision' = difficult choice. 'Final decision' is common in professional settings.

words.simpleMeaning: activity involving effort to achieve something

sentences.office: That work was completed ahead of schedule.
sentences.interview: This type of work is my passion.
sentences.business: Those work processes need optimization.
sentences.formal: This work has been duly acknowledged.

words.usageNote: 'Work' is uncountable: 'this work', not 'these works'. 'Piece of work' makes it countable. 'Hard work' pays off.

words.simpleMeaning: the indefinite continued progress of existence

sentences.daily: This time I will not make a mistake.
sentences.office: That time was very stressful.
sentences.interview: This is not the right time for that question.
sentences.business: Those times were very challenging for the industry.
sentences.formal: This time limit must be strictly followed.
sentences.informal: OK this is the last time, I promise!

words.usageNote: 'This time' = now / on this occasion. 'That time' = a past occasion. 'These days' = currently.

words.simpleMeaning: a particular area or location

sentences.office: That place is perfect for a team outing.
sentences.interview: I visited this place during a field trip.
sentences.business: Those places have high footfall.
sentences.formal: This place has been designated as a heritage zone.
sentences.informal: OMG, this place is so beautiful!

words.usageNote: 'Place' as a verb means to put something somewhere. 'This place' = specific location nearby.

words.simpleMeaning: a method or path

sentences.office: That way of working is outdated.
sentences.interview: This is the way I approach problems.
sentences.business: Those ways of marketing are no longer effective.
sentences.formal: This way ensures compliance with standards.

words.usageNote: 'This way' = direction or method. 'No way!' = absolutely not. 'By the way' = incidentally.

words.simpleMeaning: a period of 24 hours

sentences.office: That day we had an important client meeting.
sentences.interview: I still remember that day of my first interview.
sentences.business: Those days of rapid growth were exciting.
sentences.formal: This day has been declared a national holiday.
sentences.informal: This is the best day ever!

words.usageNote: 'This day' = today / specific day. 'Those days' = past times. 'Day by day' = gradually.

words.simpleMeaning: a period of 365 days

sentences.daily: This year has been very productive.
sentences.office: That year we won the best company award.
sentences.interview: This year marks five years in my career.
sentences.business: Those years of investment are finally paying off.
sentences.formal: This year's budget has been approved.
sentences.informal: This year went by so fast!

words.usageNote: 'This year' = the current year. 'Last year' vs 'that year'. 'Year after year' = consistently.

words.simpleMeaning: coins and notes used for buying things

sentences.office: That money was the project budget.
sentences.interview: This job offers good money.
sentences.business: Those monies have been transferred.
sentences.formal: This money has been allocated for infrastructure.
sentences.informal: I don't have that kind of money!

words.usageNote: 'Money' is uncountable — 'this money', not 'these money'. 'Make money', 'save money', 'spend money'.

words.simpleMeaning: a portable personal computer

sentences.office: That laptop belongs to the IT department.
sentences.interview: I brought this laptop for the presentation.
sentences.business: Those laptops have been ordered for the new team.
sentences.formal: This laptop has encrypted security software.
sentences.informal: Bro, this laptop is brand new!

words.usageNote: 'Laptop' is a countable noun. 'This laptop's battery' = possessive. 'Use this laptop' = demonstrative + noun.

words.simpleMeaning: a handheld wireless telephone

sentences.daily: This mobile has a great camera.
sentences.office: Keep your mobile on silent.
sentences.interview: Please switch off this mobile before the interview.
sentences.business: Those mobiles are company-issued devices.
sentences.formal: This mobile number is for official use only.

words.usageNote: In India 'mobile' is more commonly used than 'cell phone'. 'Mobile phone' = full form. 'Is this your mobile?' = common question.

words.simpleMeaning: a device for taking photographs

sentences.office: That camera is for security monitoring.
sentences.interview: Photography — this camera is my tool.
sentences.business: Those cameras are installed in all stores.
sentences.formal: This camera has been issued by the security department.
sentences.informal: Omg, this camera quality is insane!

words.usageNote: 'This camera' = specific nearby camera. 'Camera-shy' means uncomfortable being photographed.

words.simpleMeaning: an electronic device for watching programs

sentences.daily: This television has a very big screen.
sentences.office: That television in the lobby shows news.
sentences.interview: I have worked in television journalism.
sentences.business: Those televisions are for our electronic store.
sentences.formal: This television broadcast reached millions.
sentences.informal: That TV show is my absolute favourite!

words.usageNote: 'Watch television' vs 'watch TV'. 'This television set' is formal. In India 'TV' is used more than 'television'.

words.simpleMeaning: a long padded seat for multiple people

sentences.office: That sofa in the waiting area is new.
sentences.interview: Please sit on this sofa.
sentences.business: Those sofas were ordered from Italy.
sentences.formal: This sofa was custom-made for the boardroom.
sentences.informal: I could sleep on this sofa all day!

words.usageNote: 'Sofa' and 'couch' are interchangeable. 'This sofa set' = the complete furniture set.

words.simpleMeaning: an appliance for keeping food cold

sentences.office: That fridge in the pantry is for everyone.
sentences.interview: I'm experienced in maintaining cold-chain logistics — this fridge network is key.
sentences.business: Those fridges are used in our pharmaceutical cold chain.
sentences.formal: This refrigerator has been maintained as per food safety standards.
sentences.informal: Is there anything to eat in this fridge?

words.usageNote: 'Fridge' is informal for 'refrigerator'. 'This fridge needs to be cleaned' = demonstrative + noun.

words.simpleMeaning: a device for cooling by moving air

sentences.office: Can you switch off that fan?
sentences.interview: Please sit under this fan.
sentences.business: Those fans are from our electrical product line.
sentences.formal: This fan was installed last month.
sentences.informal: Turn on that fan, it's so hot!

words.usageNote: 'Fan' also means an admirer ('a big fan of cricket'). 'This fan doesn't work' = the appliance. 'Fan the flames' = idiom.

words.simpleMeaning: a device for measuring time

sentences.daily: This clock shows the wrong time.
sentences.office: That clock on the wall is very old.
sentences.interview: Please be aware of this clock during the test.
sentences.business: Those clocks are being supplied to schools.
sentences.formal: This clock is synchronized with the server.
sentences.informal: That clock has been wrong for weeks!

words.usageNote: 'Clock' vs 'watch': Clocks are fixed; watches are worn. 'Around the clock' = 24 hours. 'Clock in/out' = record working hours.

words.simpleMeaning: a metal device for locking or unlocking

sentences.daily: This key opens the front door.
sentences.office: That key belongs to the storeroom.
sentences.interview: This key experience prepared me well.
sentences.business: Those keys were given to the warehouse staff.
sentences.formal: This key has been assigned to the designated officer.
sentences.informal: I can't find that key anywhere!

words.usageNote: 'Key' as an adjective means very important: 'this is a key point'. 'Lose the key' vs 'lose this key'.

words.simpleMeaning: electromagnetic radiation that makes things visible

sentences.office: Turn on that light please.
sentences.interview: This room has good natural light.
sentences.business: Those lights are energy-efficient LEDs.
sentences.formal: This lighting system was installed recently.
sentences.informal: Switch off that light when you leave!

words.usageNote: 'Light' can be a noun, verb, or adjective. 'Light up' = illuminate. 'This light bulb needs changing' = specific.

words.simpleMeaning: very pleasing to look at

sentences.office: That presentation was beautifully made.
sentences.interview: This is a beautiful opportunity.
sentences.business: Those products have beautiful packaging.
sentences.formal: This building has a beautiful architectural design.
sentences.informal: OMG, this view is so beautiful!

words.usageNote: 'Beautiful' is used for visual or abstract beauty. 'This is beautiful' vs 'This is pretty' — both are positive.

words.simpleMeaning: costing a lot of money

sentences.office: That software is too expensive for our budget.
sentences.interview: This role comes with an expensive lifestyle.
sentences.business: Those raw materials are getting more expensive.
sentences.formal: This equipment is expensive but durable.
sentences.informal: That's way too expensive for me!

words.usageNote: 'Expensive' describes price. 'This is too expensive' = too costly. Opposite: 'This is cheap / affordable.'

words.simpleMeaning: low in price

sentences.daily: This vegetable is very cheap today.
sentences.office: That cheap option won't last long.
sentences.interview: I can work with this cheap but effective tool.
sentences.business: Those cheap alternatives are not reliable.
sentences.formal: This option is cost-effective, not just cheap.
sentences.informal: I bought this for so cheap!

words.usageNote: 'Cheap' can mean low cost (positive) or low quality (negative). 'This is cheap' — context determines meaning.

words.simpleMeaning: recently made or obtained, not stale

sentences.office: This idea is fresh and innovative.
sentences.interview: I'm a fresh graduate ready to contribute.
sentences.business: Those fresh products have a 3-day shelf life.
sentences.formal: This fresh approach is commendable.
sentences.informal: Try this — it tastes so fresh!

words.usageNote: 'Fresh' describes food (fresh fruits), people (fresh start), or ideas (fresh perspective). 'Are these fresh?' = common market question.

words.simpleMeaning: having existed for a long time

sentences.office: Those old files need to be archived.
sentences.interview: My old experience is very relevant here.
sentences.business: That old equipment needs replacement.
sentences.formal: This old policy has been revised.
sentences.informal: That old phone still works somehow!

words.usageNote: 'Old' for things = aged/not new. For people use 'elderly' in formal situations. 'This old method doesn't work anymore.'

words.simpleMeaning: recently made or introduced

sentences.office: That new policy was implemented last week.
sentences.interview: This is a new role for me.
sentences.business: Those new products are selling very well.
sentences.formal: This new regulation takes effect from April.

words.usageNote: 'New' before a noun means recently made. 'This is new' = it was just made/bought. 'Brand new' = completely new.

words.simpleMeaning: large in size

sentences.office: That big project is almost done.
sentences.interview: This is a big opportunity for me.
sentences.business: Those big clients are our priority.
sentences.formal: This is a big decision that requires careful thought.

words.usageNote: 'Big' is informal. 'Large' is more formal. 'This is a big deal' = very important informally.

words.simpleMeaning: little in size

sentences.office: That small startup grew into a big company.
sentences.interview: I started from this small role.
sentences.business: Those small businesses are our core customers.
sentences.formal: This small but significant change matters.
sentences.informal: Look how small that puppy is!

words.usageNote: 'Small' is formal. 'Little' adds emotion (a little kitten = cute). 'This small thing matters' = importance.

words.simpleMeaning: of a high quality or standard

sentences.office: That was a good presentation.
sentences.interview: This is a good opportunity for growth.
sentences.business: Those were good revenue numbers.
sentences.formal: This proposal is good but needs refinement.

words.usageNote: 'Good' describes quality. 'Good at something' = skilled. 'Good for you' = beneficial. 'Feels good' = positive emotion.

words.simpleMeaning: of poor quality or morally wrong

sentences.office: That was a bad decision.
sentences.interview: I learned from that bad experience.
sentences.business: Those bad reviews affected our sales.
sentences.formal: This bad practice must be stopped immediately.

words.usageNote: 'Bad' describes poor quality or negative situations. 'Too bad' = unfortunate. 'Not bad' = actually good (understatement).

words.simpleMeaning: having a high temperature

sentences.office: That hot summer made the office uncomfortable.
sentences.interview: I can handle this hot and stressful environment.
sentences.business: This hot market is very competitive.
sentences.formal: This hot issue requires immediate resolution.
sentences.informal: This coffee is too hot to drink!

words.usageNote: 'Hot' for temperature and spice. 'Hot topic' = currently popular. 'This is hot news' = very recent news.

words.simpleMeaning: having a low temperature

sentences.office: That cold AC room makes me uncomfortable.
sentences.interview: This cold calling experience helped me.
sentences.business: Those cold markets are hard to penetrate.
sentences.formal: This cold storage facility is maintained at -18°C.

words.usageNote: 'Cold' for temperature. 'Cold shoulder' = ignore someone. 'Give someone the cold treatment' = be unfriendly.

words.simpleMeaning: free from dirt or impurities

sentences.office: That clean desk policy must be followed.
sentences.interview: I keep a clean record of all my work.
sentences.business: Those clean energy solutions are in demand.
sentences.formal: This clean audit was conducted last quarter.
sentences.informal: Wow, this place is super clean!

words.usageNote: 'Clean' as verb: 'clean the house'. As adjective: 'this clean room'. 'Clean slate' = fresh start.

words.simpleMeaning: covered in dirt or impure

sentences.office: That dirty office environment affects productivity.
sentences.interview: I am ready to do the dirty work too.
sentences.business: Those dirty tactics are unethical.
sentences.formal: This dirty water is not safe for consumption.
sentences.informal: Ew, this place is so dirty!

words.usageNote: 'Dirty work' = unpleasant tasks. 'This is dirty' = needs cleaning. 'Dirty hands' = morally wrong actions.

words.simpleMeaning: of great weight

sentences.office: That heavy workload is affecting the team.
sentences.interview: This heavy project taught me time management.
sentences.business: Those heavy machines need trained operators.
sentences.formal: This heavy-duty equipment complies with safety standards.
sentences.informal: I can't carry this — it's too heavy!

words.usageNote: 'Heavy' for weight or intensity. 'Heavy rain', 'heavy workload', 'heavy heart' (feeling sad).

words.simpleMeaning: not heavy in weight

sentences.office: That light workload allows for creativity.
sentences.interview: This role is light on travel requirements.
sentences.business: Those light fabrics are popular in summer.
sentences.formal: This light version of the software is available for free.

words.usageNote: 'Light' as adjective means not heavy or not intense. Different meaning from 'light' (illumination). Context matters.

words.simpleMeaning: moving or done quickly

sentences.office: That fast internet connection is needed for video calls.
sentences.interview: I am a fast learner.
sentences.business: Those fast-growing companies are disrupting the market.
sentences.formal: This fast-track process will reduce approval time.
sentences.informal: Whoa, this bike is super fast!

words.usageNote: 'Fast' is both adjective and adverb: 'this is fast', 'he runs fast'. 'Fast food' = quick food.

words.simpleMeaning: not moving or happening quickly

sentences.office: That slow response from the team was frustrating.
sentences.interview: I am thorough, though sometimes perceived as slow.
sentences.business: Those slow-moving products need promotion.
sentences.formal: This slow process needs to be streamlined.

words.usageNote: 'Slow' as adjective (this is slow) and verb (slow down). 'Slow and steady wins the race' = proverb.

words.simpleMeaning: of great height

sentences.office: Those tall shelves need a ladder.
sentences.interview: That tall candidate impressed everyone.
sentences.business: Those tall claims need evidence.
sentences.formal: This tall structure complies with height regulations.
sentences.informal: Wow, that person is really tall!

words.usageNote: 'Tall' for people and structures. 'High' for abstract things (high salary). 'A tall order' = a very difficult task.

words.simpleMeaning: of little height or duration

sentences.office: That short meeting was very productive.
sentences.interview: This is a short but important role.
sentences.business: Those short-term goals are achievable.
sentences.formal: This short-term plan needs execution.
sentences.informal: The movie was so short, I wanted more!

words.usageNote: 'Short' for height, time, or distance. 'In short' = briefly. 'Short of' = lacking. 'This is a short cut' = faster path.

words.simpleMeaning: of great significance or value

sentences.office: That important email must be replied to today.
sentences.interview: This skill is very important for the role.
sentences.business: Those important clients must be prioritized.
sentences.formal: This important regulation takes effect immediately.
sentences.informal: Bro, this is really important, listen!

words.usageNote: 'This is important' = key information. 'It is important to...' = expressing necessity with demonstrative.

words.simpleMeaning: not easy to do or understand

sentences.office: That difficult client tests our patience.
sentences.interview: This difficult situation taught me a lot.
sentences.business: Those difficult market conditions affected revenue.
sentences.formal: This difficult decision was made after careful deliberation.
sentences.informal: This maths problem is way too difficult!

words.usageNote: 'Difficult' is more formal than 'hard'. 'This is difficult for me' = personal challenge. 'Difficult person' = hard to deal with.

words.simpleMeaning: done without great effort

sentences.office: That easy shortcut saves us time.
sentences.interview: This role is not easy but I'm prepared.
sentences.business: Those easy wins build team morale.
sentences.formal: This easy-to-use system is now available.
sentences.informal: This game is so easy once you get it!

words.usageNote: 'Easy' as adjective and adverb. 'Take it easy' = relax. 'Easy does it' = be careful. 'This is easy for you' = demonstrative use.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling pleasure and contentment

sentences.office: This happy work environment boosts productivity.
sentences.interview: I am happy to be here today.
sentences.business: Those happy customers leave positive reviews.
sentences.formal: This happy occasion calls for a celebration.
sentences.informal: I'm so happy about this news!

words.usageNote: 'Happy' + about/with: 'I am happy about this'. 'Happy with this result' = satisfied. 'Happy to help' = willing.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling unhappy or sorrowful

sentences.office: That sad resignation letter made everyone emotional.
sentences.interview: That sad experience motivated me to change careers.
sentences.business: Those sad figures indicate a drop in revenue.
sentences.formal: This sad incident is deeply regrettable.
sentences.informal: That movie made me so sad!

words.usageNote: 'This is sad' = unfortunate. 'Feeling sad about this' = emotional. 'Sad state of affairs' = disappointing situation.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling strong displeasure

sentences.office: That angry response from the client surprised us.
sentences.interview: I don't get angry in this kind of situation.
sentences.business: Those angry customers need prompt resolution.
sentences.formal: This angry communication needs to be de-escalated.
sentences.informal: Why are you so angry about this?

words.usageNote: 'Angry at/with someone', 'angry about something'. 'This made me angry' = demonstrative as cause.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling fear or anxiety

sentences.daily: I am afraid of this dog.
sentences.office: That afraid-of-failure mindset holds us back.
sentences.interview: I am not afraid of this challenge.
sentences.business: Those afraid investors pulled out of the market.
sentences.formal: This is not something to be afraid of.

words.usageNote: 'Afraid of + noun/gerund': 'afraid of this dog', 'afraid of making mistakes'. 'I'm afraid' = polite apology.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling a need for rest

sentences.office: This tired team needs a break.
sentences.interview: I never get tired of learning.
sentences.business: Those tired marketing strategies need updating.
sentences.formal: This tired workforce needs adequate rest.
sentences.informal: I'm so tired of this commute!

words.usageNote: 'Tired of + noun/gerund' = fed up: 'tired of this work'. 'Tired' from exertion vs 'tired of' = bored/frustrated.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling a desire for food

sentences.office: Is anyone else hungry in this meeting?
sentences.interview: I am hungry for success.
sentences.business: Those hungry entrepreneurs are disrupting the market.
sentences.formal: This hunger for knowledge is commendable.

words.usageNote: 'Hungry for' = strongly desiring (hungry for success). 'Are you hungry?' = common daily question. 'This makes me hungry' = demonstrative use.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling a desire for water

sentences.office: Can I have some water? I'm thirsty.
sentences.interview: I'm thirsty for challenges.
sentences.business: Those thirsty markets are ready for our products.
sentences.formal: This warm weather makes everyone thirsty.
sentences.informal: I'm so thirsty after this workout!

words.usageNote: 'Thirsty for' = eager for: 'thirsty for knowledge'. 'Is this water?' — a common demonstrative question when thirsty.

words.simpleMeaning: to present someone for the first time

sentences.office: This is how I introduced myself in the meeting.
sentences.interview: Let me introduce myself.
sentences.business: This introduction helped us win the client.
sentences.formal: May I introduce this distinguished guest?
sentences.informal: Hey, let me introduce you to this amazing person!

words.usageNote: 'Introduce' uses 'This is...' construction. 'This is my friend' = standard introduction. 'Let me introduce this person to you.'

words.simpleMeaning: to indicate direction or location

sentences.daily: He is pointing at that building.
sentences.office: This point is very important.
sentences.interview: Can you point to your strengths?
sentences.business: Those data points confirm the trend.
sentences.formal: This point was raised in the last meeting.

words.usageNote: 'Point to/at' something = use finger/gesture. 'This is a good point' = valid argument. 'At this point' = at this stage.

words.simpleMeaning: to recognize who or what something is

sentences.office: This problem has been identified.
sentences.interview: I can identify this as a leadership challenge.
sentences.business: Those issues were identified in the audit.
sentences.formal: This risk has been identified and mitigated.

words.usageNote: 'Identify this/that' uses demonstratives. 'Identify + noun' = recognize it. 'Identify with someone' = relate to them.

words.simpleMeaning: to pick out or select

sentences.office: That was the option we chose.
sentences.interview: I choose this company for its values.
sentences.business: Those clients chose our product over competitors.
sentences.formal: This option was chosen after careful evaluation.

words.usageNote: 'Choose this' vs 'choose that' — demonstrative helps with selection. 'Choose between this and that' = decide.

words.simpleMeaning: to examine the similarities and differences

sentences.office: This report compares both options.
sentences.interview: Let me compare this role with my past experience.
sentences.business: Those prices are incomparable.
sentences.formal: This comparison was made using standardized metrics.
sentences.informal: Don't even compare this to that!

words.usageNote: 'Compare this to/with that' = use both demonstratives. 'Beyond compare' = unmatched quality.

words.simpleMeaning: close in distance

sentences.office: That near-completion project will be done soon.
sentences.interview: My home is near this office.
sentences.business: Those near-future plans are already in motion.
sentences.formal: This near-disaster situation was averted.
sentences.informal: The station is near, don't worry!

words.usageNote: 'Near' as adjective: 'this is near'. As preposition: 'near this building'. 'Near and dear' = close loved ones.

words.simpleMeaning: at a great distance

sentences.office: That far-reaching policy affects everyone.
sentences.interview: How far is this office from the metro?
sentences.business: Those far-off markets require a different strategy.
sentences.formal: This decision will have far-reaching consequences.
sentences.informal: That's way too far to walk!

words.usageNote: 'Far' vs 'far away': both correct. 'This is far from ideal' = not at all ideal (idiom). 'How far is that?' = distance question.

words.simpleMeaning: in or at this place

sentences.office: We work here.
sentences.interview: I am here for the interview.
sentences.business: Our branch is here.
sentences.formal: Please place your signature here.

words.usageNote: 'Here' = this place. 'This is here' = awkward; just say 'here'. 'Here and now' = the present situation.

words.simpleMeaning: in or at that place

sentences.office: The conference room is there.
sentences.interview: My last company was there in Pune.
sentences.business: Our warehouse is there.
sentences.formal: Please proceed to the office over there.

words.usageNote: 'There' = that place. 'There is/are' = existence structure. 'There you go' = presenting something to someone.

words.simpleMeaning: having great beauty

sentences.office: That beautiful design won an award.
sentences.interview: This beautiful opportunity came at the right time.
sentences.business: Those beautiful packaging designs increased sales.
sentences.formal: This beautiful monument is a tourist attraction.

words.usageNote: 'Beautiful' for visual beauty or abstract appreciation. 'This is a beautiful idea' = wonderful concept.

words.simpleMeaning: likely to cause harm

sentences.office: That dangerous shortcut in the process caused errors.
sentences.interview: I can handle this dangerous assignment.
sentences.business: Those dangerous chemicals require special handling.
sentences.formal: This dangerous area has been cordoned off.

words.usageNote: 'Dangerous' + for/to: 'dangerous for children', 'dangerous to health'. 'This is dangerous' = specific risk.

words.simpleMeaning: protected from harm or danger

sentences.daily: Is this water safe to drink?
sentences.office: That safe environment helps employees be productive.
sentences.interview: I ensure this data is kept safe.
sentences.business: Those safe practices are industry standards.
sentences.formal: This safe route has been recommended.
sentences.informal: Are you safe? That storm looks bad!

words.usageNote: 'Is this safe?' = A1 demonstrative safety question. 'Safe and sound' = completely safe (idiom).

words.simpleMeaning: right or without errors

sentences.office: That correct procedure must be followed.
sentences.interview: This is the correct approach to the problem.
sentences.business: Those figures are now correct.
sentences.formal: This correct version replaces the previous one.

words.usageNote: 'Is this correct?' = A1 demonstrative question. 'Correct me if I'm wrong' = inviting feedback.

words.simpleMeaning: not right or correct

sentences.office: That wrong assumption led to a bad decision.
sentences.interview: I admit when I am wrong.
sentences.business: Those wrong calculations cost us money.
sentences.formal: This wrong information has been corrected.

words.usageNote: 'Wrong' as adjective (this is wrong) and verb (to wrong someone = to treat unjustly). 'What's wrong with this?' = demonstrative question.

words.simpleMeaning: to make an effort to do something

sentences.office: Let's try this new approach.
sentences.interview: I always try this method first.
sentences.business: Those markets have never been tried.
sentences.formal: This method was tried and tested.

words.usageNote: 'Try this' = offering something to taste/experience. 'Try out this new thing' = test it. 'Try and fail' = not succeed but make effort.

words.simpleMeaning: to grasp the meaning of something

sentences.office: Do you understand this process?
sentences.interview: I completely understand this requirement.
sentences.business: Those customers didn't understand that product.
sentences.formal: This policy must be understood by all.
sentences.informal: I don't understand that at all!

words.usageNote: 'Do you understand this?' = common question. 'I understand' vs 'I understand this' — adding demonstrative is more specific.

words.simpleMeaning: to recall something from the past

sentences.daily: I remember that day very well.
sentences.office: Remember this rule.
sentences.interview: I remember that key project clearly.
sentences.business: Those customers still remember this brand.
sentences.formal: Please remember this important instruction.
sentences.informal: Do you remember that time when...?

words.usageNote: 'Remember this' = command to keep in mind. 'I remember that' = specific past memory with demonstrative.

words.simpleMeaning: to fail to remember

sentences.office: I forgot that meeting.
sentences.interview: I never forget this kind of lesson.
sentences.business: Those opportunities should not be forgotten.
sentences.formal: Please do not forget this deadline.
sentences.informal: Forget about that, it's not important!

words.usageNote: 'Forget this' = stop thinking about it. 'Don't forget this' = remember it. 'Forgive and forget' = let go.

words.simpleMeaning: to require something essential

sentences.office: This project needs more resources.
sentences.interview: This role needs strong communication skills.
sentences.business: Those markets need localized strategies.
sentences.formal: This document needs your approval.

words.usageNote: 'Need this' = immediate necessity. 'This needs to be done' = task requirement. 'I need that = wanting something specific.

words.simpleMeaning: to desire or wish for something

sentences.office: What do you want from this meeting?
sentences.interview: I want this job very much.
sentences.business: Those clients want a better deal.
sentences.formal: This proposal contains what both parties want.

words.usageNote: 'Want this' = desire a specific thing. 'Want + this/that + noun'. 'Do you want this?' = offering something.

words.simpleMeaning: to find something pleasant

sentences.office: That method is something I like.
sentences.interview: I like this company's culture.
sentences.business: Those customers like our new product.
sentences.formal: This initiative is one that I wholeheartedly like.

words.usageNote: 'Like this' = like the nearby thing. 'I like this better than that' = comparison using demonstratives.

words.simpleMeaning: to find something unpleasant

sentences.office: That disliked policy was finally changed.
sentences.interview: I dislike this kind of disorganized approach.
sentences.business: Those disliked products were discontinued.
sentences.formal: This dislike for the process needs to be addressed.
sentences.informal: Ugh, I dislike that so much!

words.usageNote: 'Dislike this' = specific thing you don't like. More formal than 'I don't like this'.

words.simpleMeaning: to keep something in hand

sentences.office: This project holds great potential.
sentences.interview: This experience holds me in good stead.
sentences.business: Those products hold 30% of the market share.
sentences.formal: This agreement holds legal validity.
sentences.informal: Can you hold this for me?

words.usageNote: 'Hold this' = physical request. 'Hold on' = wait. 'Hold a meeting' = conduct. 'This position holds promise' = metaphorical.

words.simpleMeaning: to transfer something to someone

sentences.office: Give this report to the manager.
sentences.interview: I can give this role my 100%.
sentences.business: Those investors will give this startup funding.
sentences.formal: This has been given due consideration.

words.usageNote: 'Give this to' = specific object. 'Give + direct object + indirect object'. 'Give it a try' = attempt something.

words.simpleMeaning: to reach for and hold something

sentences.office: Take this opportunity.
sentences.interview: Take this challenge as a stepping stone.
sentences.business: Those decisions were taken hastily.
sentences.formal: This matter will take time to resolve.

words.usageNote: 'Take this' = accept something offered. 'Take that road' = direction. 'Take this seriously' = treat with importance.

words.simpleMeaning: to move something to a place

sentences.office: Put that file in this drawer.
sentences.interview: Let me put this simply.
sentences.business: Those plans were put on hold.
sentences.formal: This proposal has been put forward.

words.usageNote: 'Put this here' = physical placement with demonstrative. 'Put on hold' = delay. 'Put in a good word' = recommend.

words.simpleMeaning: to carry something to a place

sentences.office: Bring that document to the meeting.
sentences.interview: This experience brings a lot to the table.
sentences.business: Those new markets will bring revenue.
sentences.formal: This proposal brings several benefits.

words.usageNote: 'Bring this here' = toward the speaker. 'Take that there' = away from speaker. Common confusion to clarify.

words.simpleMeaning: to move so as to unblock an access

sentences.office: This new project opens many possibilities.
sentences.interview: I am open to this challenge.
sentences.business: Those markets are open for entry.
sentences.formal: This session is now open.
sentences.informal: Can you open that for me?

words.usageNote: 'Open this' = imperative. 'Is this open?' = question. 'Open to this idea' = receptive. 'This door is open' = available.

words.simpleMeaning: to move so as to cover an opening

sentences.office: That deal was closed last week.
sentences.interview: I want to close this chapter of my career.
sentences.business: Those stores are closed on Sundays.
sentences.formal: This matter has been closed.

words.usageNote: 'Close this' = shut it. 'Is this closed?' = question. 'Close this deal' = finalize it in business context.

words.simpleMeaning: to display or make visible

sentences.office: This report shows a 20% increase.
sentences.interview: Let me show this to you.
sentences.business: Those numbers show strong performance.
sentences.formal: This diagram shows the process flow.

words.usageNote: 'Show this to me' = present. 'Show + person + this/that'. 'Show and tell' = demonstrate and explain.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something clear

sentences.office: This diagram explains the process.
sentences.interview: Let me explain this situation.
sentences.business: Those terms need to be explained clearly.
sentences.formal: This concept will be explained in detail.

words.usageNote: 'Explain this to me' = clarify. Cannot say 'explain me this' — must say 'explain this to me'.

words.simpleMeaning: to be aware of or understand something

sentences.office: I know this system well.
sentences.interview: I know how to handle this.
sentences.business: Those customers don't know about this product.
sentences.formal: This information is known to all parties.

words.usageNote: 'Know this' = specific knowledge. 'Do you know that?' = question. 'You know what?' = introducing something.

words.simpleMeaning: to perceive with the eyes

sentences.office: I see this project as a challenge.
sentences.interview: I see this role as a great fit.
sentences.business: Those investors want to see this product.
sentences.formal: This issue needs to be seen from multiple perspectives.
sentences.informal: Do you see that over there?

words.usageNote: 'See this' = perceive it visually. 'I see' = I understand. 'See that' + pointing = look at the distant thing.

words.simpleMeaning: to perceive sound with the ears

sentences.office: I heard this news in the morning.
sentences.interview: I heard this about your company.
sentences.business: Those clients need to hear this pitch.
sentences.formal: This complaint has been heard.

words.usageNote: 'Hear' = passive reception of sound. 'Listen' = active. 'Did you hear that?' = sudden sound. 'Hear this' = pay attention.

words.simpleMeaning: to experience a sensation or emotion

sentences.office: This situation feels overwhelming.
sentences.interview: I feel this job is perfect for me.
sentences.business: Those customers feel this product is overpriced.
sentences.formal: This grievance is deeply felt.

words.usageNote: 'Feel + adjective': 'this feels right'. 'Feel about this' = opinion. 'How do you feel about that?' = asking for reaction.

words.simpleMeaning: to form ideas in the mind

sentences.office: Think about this proposal.
sentences.interview: I think this role suits me.
sentences.business: Those customers think this price is too high.
sentences.formal: This conclusion was reached after careful thinking.
sentences.informal: I think that's a great idea!

words.usageNote: 'I think this is right' = opinion with demonstrative. 'Think about this' = contemplate. 'Think twice about that' = reconsider.

words.simpleMeaning: to speak words

sentences.daily: What did you say about this?
sentences.office: I said this in the meeting.
sentences.interview: I always say this in interviews.
sentences.business: Those experts say this market is growing.
sentences.formal: This statement was officially said by the director.

words.usageNote: 'Say this' = express a specific thing. 'They say that...' = common belief. 'What do you say about this?' = opinion question.

words.simpleMeaning: to request information or something

sentences.office: Ask about this in the meeting.
sentences.interview: May I ask this question?
sentences.business: Those customers are asking about this feature.
sentences.formal: This query was asked by the committee.
sentences.informal: Can I ask you something about that?

words.usageNote: 'Ask this question' = specific question. 'Ask about this' = inquire. 'Ask for this' = request it.

words.simpleMeaning: to reply to a question

sentences.office: That answer was very clear.
sentences.interview: I answered this question confidently.
sentences.business: Those queries were answered promptly.
sentences.formal: This question has been duly answered.

words.usageNote: 'Answer this' = respond to specific question. 'This is the answer' = the correct response. 'Answer to this' = solution.

words.simpleMeaning: to assist someone

sentences.daily: Can you help me with this?
sentences.office: This resource will help the project.
sentences.interview: This skill helps me do my job better.
sentences.business: Those tools help us serve customers better.
sentences.formal: This assistance is greatly appreciated.
sentences.informal: Can you help me with that?

words.usageNote: 'Help with this' = assist with specific task. 'Can't help this' = can't avoid it. 'It can't be helped' = unavoidable.

words.simpleMeaning: to get something in exchange for money

sentences.office: The company bought that software.
sentences.interview: I bought into this company's vision.
sentences.business: Those clients bought our premium package.
sentences.formal: This asset was bought at market value.

words.usageNote: 'Buy this' = purchase the specific thing. 'I'll buy that' (informal) = I believe you. 'Buy into' = believe or invest.

words.simpleMeaning: to exchange something for money

sentences.office: This product sells very well.
sentences.interview: I sold this concept to management.
sentences.business: Those products are selling faster than expected.
sentences.formal: This asset will be sold at auction.

words.usageNote: 'Sell this' = offer for money. 'Sell an idea' = convince someone. 'Sell out' = betray or sell all stock.

words.simpleMeaning: to employ something for a purpose

sentences.office: We use this software daily.
sentences.interview: I used to use this system at my previous job.
sentences.business: Those machines are used in manufacturing.
sentences.formal: This tool is used for data analysis.
sentences.informal: You can use that if you want!

words.usageNote: 'Use this' = employ specific object. 'Used to' = past habit (different meaning). 'Use this wisely' = good advice.

words.simpleMeaning: to create or produce something

sentences.office: This decision was made yesterday.
sentences.interview: I made this project successful.
sentences.business: Those products are made in India.
sentences.formal: This policy was made to protect employees.
sentences.informal: Can you make that for me?

words.usageNote: 'Make this' = create specific thing. 'Make a decision' = decide. 'Make a mistake' = err. 'This makes sense' = is logical.

words.simpleMeaning: to perform an action

sentences.office: This task needs to be done today.
sentences.interview: I can do this job well.
sentences.business: Those deals were done last quarter.
sentences.formal: This has been done as per protocol.

words.usageNote: 'Do this' = complete specific task. 'Do it' = complete (replacing noun). 'This won't do' = not acceptable.

words.simpleMeaning: to move toward the speaker

sentences.office: This opportunity came at the right time.
sentences.interview: I came prepared for this.
sentences.business: Those clients will come next week.
sentences.formal: This delegation will come on Monday.

words.usageNote: 'Come here' = approach speaker. 'Come to this' = reach a stage. 'This comes naturally' = is easy for me.

words.simpleMeaning: to move away from the speaker

sentences.office: Let's go with this option.
sentences.interview: I want to go this direction in my career.
sentences.business: Those products go to the international market.
sentences.formal: This proposal will go to management for approval.

words.usageNote: 'Go there' = move away. 'Go with this plan' = choose it. 'This is going well' = progressing nicely.

words.simpleMeaning: to rest with the body supported on a seat

sentences.office: Please sit in this chair.
sentences.interview: Please sit here for the interview.
sentences.business: Those clients were asked to sit in that conference room.
sentences.formal: Please be seated in this designated area.

words.usageNote: 'Sit here/there' = direction with demonstrative. 'Sit on this' = on top of. 'Sit in on this meeting' = attend as observer.

words.simpleMeaning: to be in an upright position

sentences.office: This company stands for quality.
sentences.interview: I stand by this decision.
sentences.business: Those products stand out in the market.
sentences.formal: This stand was taken after careful consideration.

words.usageNote: 'Stand here' = be at this location. 'Stand for this' = represent. 'Can't stand this' = hate it. 'Stand by this' = support it.

words.simpleMeaning: to remain in readiness for something

sentences.office: This decision will have to wait.
sentences.interview: I can wait for this opportunity.
sentences.business: Those orders are waiting to be processed.
sentences.formal: This matter is currently in waiting.

words.usageNote: 'Wait here/there' = stay at this location. 'This can wait' = not urgent. 'Wait for this' = anticipate something specific.

words.simpleMeaning: to show or give something to someone formally

sentences.daily: This is a present for you.
sentences.office: Please present this data.
sentences.interview: I will present this idea during the meeting.
sentences.business: Those managers will present this proposal.
sentences.formal: This report is being presented today.
sentences.informal: I have a surprise — this is your present!

words.usageNote: 'Present this' = show/give formally. 'This is a present' = gift. 'At this present time' = right now.

words.simpleMeaning: to get something given or sent

sentences.office: Have you received that email?
sentences.interview: I received this offer yesterday.
sentences.business: Those clients received this product last week.
sentences.formal: This award was received with great honor.

words.usageNote: 'Receive this' = get a specific thing. 'This was received well' = had positive response. 'On the receiving end' = being affected by something.

words.simpleMeaning: to cause something to go somewhere

sentences.office: Please send this report today.
sentences.interview: I sent this email as a follow-up.
sentences.business: Those packages were sent to all branches.
sentences.formal: This letter has been sent to all stakeholders.

words.usageNote: 'Send this to' = dispatch specific item to someone. 'This has been sent' = passive. 'Send someone this' = give them specific thing.

words.simpleMeaning: to understand written words

sentences.office: Please read this report carefully.
sentences.interview: I read this company's history.
sentences.business: Those clients have read this brochure.
sentences.formal: This document must be read before signing.
sentences.informal: Read that when you get a chance!

words.usageNote: 'Read this' = read the specific thing. Note: past tense 'read' is pronounced /rɛd/ not /riːd/. 'Read between the lines' = understand hidden meaning.

words.simpleMeaning: to form letters or words on a surface

sentences.office: Write this in the meeting minutes.
sentences.interview: I wrote this cover letter myself.
sentences.business: Those emails were written by the marketing team.
sentences.formal: This letter must be written formally.
sentences.informal: Write that down before you forget!

words.usageNote: 'Write this down' = note it. 'This is written' = passive. 'Write off this debt' = cancel financially.

words.simpleMeaning: to say words aloud

sentences.office: This speaker spoke very confidently.
sentences.interview: Let me speak about this experience.
sentences.business: Those clients speak highly of this product.
sentences.formal: This was spoken at the annual conference.
sentences.informal: Can I speak to that guy?

words.usageNote: 'Speak about this' = discuss topic. 'Speak to this person' = address them. 'Speak up' = talk louder or say something.

words.simpleMeaning: to pay attention to sound

sentences.office: Listen to this feedback.
sentences.interview: I'm a good listener — I listen to this kind of feedback carefully.
sentences.business: Those customers will listen to this pitch.
sentences.formal: This must be listened to with full attention.

words.usageNote: 'Listen to this' = pay attention to specific thing. 'Listen' needs 'to': 'listen to this', not just 'listen this'.

words.simpleMeaning: to gain knowledge or skill

sentences.office: I learned this on the job.
sentences.interview: I'm eager to learn this skill.
sentences.business: Those employees learned this tool quickly.
sentences.formal: This lesson was learned the hard way.
sentences.informal: You should learn that, it's useful!

words.usageNote: 'Learn this' = acquire this specific knowledge. 'Learn from this mistake' = improve from experience. 'Learn + noun' not 'learn about + noun' in basic sense.

words.simpleMeaning: to help someone learn something

sentences.office: Can you teach me this process?
sentences.interview: I can teach this to new team members.
sentences.business: Those trainers taught this skill effectively.
sentences.formal: This was taught as part of the curriculum.

words.usageNote: 'Teach someone this' = show how. 'Teach + person + noun'. 'This teaches a lesson' = provides a learning.

words.simpleMeaning: to engage in activity for enjoyment

sentences.office: This company plays an important role.
sentences.interview: I played a key role in this project.
sentences.business: Those companies are playing it safe.
sentences.formal: This sector plays a vital role in the economy.
sentences.informal: Come play that game with us!

words.usageNote: 'Play this game' = engage with specific game. 'Play a role' = have a function. 'Play it by ear' = improvise.

words.simpleMeaning: to move quickly or to manage something

sentences.office: Who runs this department?
sentences.interview: I ran this project for six months.
sentences.business: Those businesses run very efficiently.
sentences.formal: This program runs on all devices.

words.usageNote: 'Run this' = manage something. 'Run toward this' = physical movement. 'Run out of this' = use up completely.

words.simpleMeaning: to move at a regular pace on foot

sentences.office: This building is walking distance away.
sentences.interview: It's a five-minute walk from this metro station.
sentences.business: Those clients walked out of the meeting.
sentences.formal: This area is designated as a walking zone.
sentences.informal: Let's walk over to that place!

words.usageNote: 'Walk this way' = follow me. 'A ten-minute walk from this' = nearby. 'Walk the talk' = practice what you preach.

words.simpleMeaning: to put food in the mouth and swallow it

sentences.office: I eat at this canteen daily.
sentences.interview: I don't eat much during this kind of stressful time.
sentences.business: Those employees eat at this cafeteria.
sentences.formal: This food was eaten by all the guests.

words.usageNote: 'Eat this' = consume specific food. 'This is not eaten' = cultural avoidance. 'Eat in or out' = at home or restaurant.

words.simpleMeaning: to swallow liquid

sentences.office: Can I have this to drink?
sentences.interview: I don't drink this kind of tea.
sentences.business: Those meetings always have drinks available.
sentences.formal: This beverage was served to all attendees.
sentences.informal: Drink that before it gets cold!

words.usageNote: 'Drink this' = consume the specific liquid. 'Drink in' = absorb (drink in the view). 'Soft drink' vs 'drink' (alcohol).

words.simpleMeaning: to rest the mind and body in sleep

sentences.office: This tight deadline barely left time to sleep.
sentences.interview: I don't sleep well the night before this kind of interview.
sentences.business: Those night shifts don't allow much sleep.
sentences.formal: This rest period is mandated by labor law.
sentences.informal: I could sleep on that sofa all day!

words.usageNote: 'Sleep here' = location. 'I'll sleep on this' = think overnight before deciding (idiom).

words.simpleMeaning: to remove dirt from something

sentences.office: This area must be cleaned before the meeting.
sentences.interview: I clean my work area every day.
sentences.business: Those warehouses were cleaned last month.
sentences.formal: This workspace must be maintained clean.
sentences.informal: Can you clean that up please?

words.usageNote: 'Clean this' = verb action. 'This is clean' = adjective state. Both forms are important.

words.simpleMeaning: to repair or solve a problem

sentences.office: This error needs to be fixed.
sentences.interview: I fixed this issue in my previous role.
sentences.business: Those bugs were fixed in the latest update.
sentences.formal: This defect has been fixed as per quality standards.
sentences.informal: Can you fix that for me?

words.usageNote: 'Fix this' = repair specific thing. 'Quick fix' = temporary solution. 'This is fixed' = resolved (passive).

words.simpleMeaning: to damage something so it no longer works

sentences.office: This rule must not be broken.
sentences.interview: I never break this kind of deadline.
sentences.business: Those deadlines were broken by the previous team.
sentences.formal: This contract cannot be broken without penalties.

words.usageNote: 'Break this' = damage. 'Break the rules' = violate them. 'This is broken' = not working. 'Take a break from this' = rest.

words.simpleMeaning: to make or become different

sentences.office: This process needs to change.
sentences.interview: I changed this entire system.
sentences.business: Those market conditions changed rapidly.
sentences.formal: This policy has been changed effective immediately.

words.usageNote: 'Change this' = modify specific thing. 'This changes everything' = big impact. 'Change of this' = modification.

words.simpleMeaning: to come to an end or cause to end

sentences.office: Stop this process immediately.
sentences.interview: I stopped this inefficiency in my last job.
sentences.business: Those losses need to be stopped.
sentences.formal: This activity must be stopped immediately.

words.usageNote: 'Stop this' = end specific action. 'Stop + gerund' = quit doing. 'This has to stop' = strong insistence.

words.simpleMeaning: to begin doing something

sentences.office: When does this project start?
sentences.interview: I want to start this new chapter.
sentences.business: Those operations start next quarter.
sentences.formal: This initiative will officially start tomorrow.

words.usageNote: 'Start this' = begin specific thing. 'Start + gerund or infinitive' both work. 'From this point, start...' = from now.

words.simpleMeaning: to complete something

sentences.office: This report must be finished by noon.
sentences.interview: I finished this project ahead of schedule.
sentences.business: Those orders will be finished by Friday.
sentences.formal: This project has been finished successfully.

words.usageNote: 'Finish this' = complete specific task. 'Finished with this' = done with it. 'Finish + gerund' (not infinitive): 'finish doing this'.

words.simpleMeaning: to carry on without stopping

sentences.office: This work must continue.
sentences.interview: I want to continue this career path.
sentences.business: Those operations will continue next year.
sentences.formal: This program will continue as planned.

words.usageNote: 'Continue this' = keep doing specific thing. 'Continue with this' = proceed. 'Continued support for this' = ongoing.

words.simpleMeaning: to change position or place

sentences.office: This team moved to the new floor.
sentences.interview: I am ready to move to this city.
sentences.business: Those operations will move online.
sentences.formal: This office will be moved to a new location.
sentences.informal: Move that out of the way!

words.usageNote: 'Move this here/there' = relocate. 'This moves me' = emotionally affects. 'Move forward with this' = proceed.

words.simpleMeaning: to retain or maintain

sentences.office: Keep this information confidential.
sentences.interview: I keep this principle in mind always.
sentences.business: Those customers are kept informed.
sentences.formal: This record must be kept for five years.

words.usageNote: 'Keep this' = retain specific thing. 'Keep + adjective': 'keep this safe'. 'Keep in mind' = remember. 'Keep it up' = continue well.

words.simpleMeaning: to go away or to let remain

sentences.office: Leave this document on my desk.
sentences.interview: I am planning to leave this company.
sentences.business: Those clients left this feedback.
sentences.formal: This area must be left undisturbed.
sentences.informal: Just leave that, I'll handle it!

words.usageNote: 'Leave this' = go away from / let remain. 'Leave a message' = leave behind. 'Leave this to me' = let me handle it.

words.simpleMeaning: to choose or to take up something

sentences.office: Pick this option.
sentences.interview: I was picked for this project.
sentences.business: Those clients picked our service.
sentences.formal: This candidate was picked after careful selection.

words.usageNote: 'Pick this' = choose/lift. 'Pick up this' = collect. 'Pick apart this idea' = critique. 'Cherry-pick' = choose only the best.

words.simpleMeaning: to hold and take from one place to another

sentences.office: This bag carries all the documents.
sentences.interview: I carry this experience with me.
sentences.business: Those trucks carry our products.
